topics: 🧱 Standing still in רשות הרבים = becoming a קֶרֶן זָוִית
🏃 Yose HaBavli: a runner in public is liable, but exempt at twilight on Erev Shabbat
🪓 The wood-splitter — chips that fly into a worker's eye
🏠 Inviting someone in: does the courtyard become שׁוּתָּפוּת?
🐂 Two oxen wound each other — paying בַּמּוֹתָר (the difference)

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Stopping, Running, and Splitting Wood
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The daf strings together a series of refinements. Rav says the rule of the standing beam-carrier only applies when he מְמַלָּא כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים. Yose HaBavli rules that running is itself a deviation that creates liability — except at twilight on Erev Shabbat, when running is normal. The wood-splitter case asks whether saying 'come in' creates a partnership-courtyard. Then the chapter shifts to two oxen wounding each other and the calculation of הַמּוֹתָר.

slides.title: 💬 The Standing Walker — Like a Street Corner
slides.subtitle: Rav's qualification of the Mishnah's rule.

lines.speaker: Rav
lines.text: The Mishnah's liability for stopping only applies בִּמְמַלָּא כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים — when his beam blocks the entire road.

lines.speaker: Q
lines.text: But our Mishnah's beam-carrier did not necessarily fill the whole road — so why is he liable for stopping?

lines.speaker: A
lines.text: כֵּיוָן שֶׁעָמַד נַעֲשֶׂה כְקֶרֶן זָוִית — the moment he stopped, he became a 'street corner' — a fixed obstacle the next walker can't anticipate.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: Walking and stopping are categorically different. A pedestrian flow allows reaction; a sudden corner does not.

callout.label: Key concept:
callout.text: קֶרֶן זָוִית is the legal image: a stationary projection into a moving stream. That image converts a pedestrian into a hazard.

slides.title: 🏃 Yose HaBavli — The Running Pedestrian
slides.subtitle: When ordinary public-domain motion crosses into deviation.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Mishnah: two running, or one running one walking — if they damage each other, both exempt.
2️⃣ Yose HaBavli pushes back: הָיָה רָץ בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים וְהִזִּיק חַיָיב — running is a deviation from the norm.
3️⃣ שֶׁשִּׁינָּה הַמִּנְהָג — once you broke from normal pace, you assumed the risk.
4️⃣ Exception: עֶרֶב שַׁבָּת בֵּין הַשְּׁמָשׁוֹת — twilight on Friday, running to finish before Shabbat is itself the norm.
5️⃣ Aside: Yose HaBavli is identified as Yose ben Yehudah, also known as Yose Katontah — שֶׁהָיָה קְטָן חֲסִידִים, the youngest/last of the pious.

callout.label: Halakhic move:
callout.text: Liability tracks מִנְהָג. Whatever a society normally does in a space sets the baseline; deviating from it transfers responsibility.

slides.title: 🪓 The Wood-Splitter and the Worker
slides.subtitle: R' Yose ben Chanina's case opens a layered debate.

boxes.title: 👁️ R' Yose ben Chanina
items: But exempt from גלות if worker dies

boxes.title: 📕 R' Chiyya's Beraita
items: R' Yose: when he saw him
R' Chiyya: when he didn't see

boxes.title: 🤝 "Come In" Theory
items: Partners are mutually liable for damages
R' Yochanan in name of R' Yannai

callout.label: The deeper move:
callout.text: Inviting someone in is a halakhic act. It transforms a private courtyard into a partnership space, importing the rule that partners owe each other for damages.

slides.title: 🐂 Two Oxen — Paying the Difference (בַּמּוֹתָר)
slides.subtitle: How the Mishnah computes mutual goring damage.

nodes.content:
Two oxen wound each other
What does each owe?

columns.label: Both תָם (untrained)
columns.content:
Pay בַּמּוֹתָר חֲצִי נֶזֶק
half of the net difference

columns.label: Both מוּעָד (forewarned)
columns.content:
Pay בַּמּוֹתָר נֶזֶק שָׁלֵם
full damages on the difference

nodes.content: R' Akiva: even a tam goring a human pays full damages on the מוֹתָר.
Computation: subtract the smaller liability from the larger; the net is what changes hands.

callout.label: Why מוֹתָר math:
callout.text: When both parties owe each other, justice requires netting — not gross payment. The Mishnah treats mutual injury as a single accounting problem.

slides.title: 📐 R' Meir vs. R' Yehudah — The Worthless Carcass
slides.subtitle: How to read וּמָכְרוּ אֶת הַשּׁוֹר הַחַי.
rows: Bull worth 200 gores bull worth 200, carcass worth 0
Sell the live bull, split proceeds (וּמָכְרוּ אֶת הַשּׁוֹר הַחַי וְחָצוּ אֶת כַּסְפּוֹ)
Same — but you ignored וְגַם אֶת הַמֵּת יֶחֱצוּן
Each takes half live + half dead — fulfills both clauses of the verse
Each phrase teaches an independent halakhah
מָאי טַעֲמָא דְּרִבִּי מֵאִיר: derives only from הַחַי
מִיכָּן שֶׁזֶּה מַפְסִיד חֲצִי נֶזֶק וְזֶה מַפְסִיד חֲצִי נֶזֶק

callout.label: The exegetical fight:
callout.text: R' Yehudah's critique is sharp: קִיַימְתָּה...וְלֹא קִיַימְתָּה — you upheld one half of the pasuk and ignored the other. Each phrase must do work.

summary.principle: Liability in the public domain hinges on whether one's behavior matches expected norms — stopping, running, or veering all transfer risk. In animal-vs-animal damages, the Mishnah nets liabilities and pays only the difference.

flowSteps.content:
Public domain incident
collision, ricochet, or mutual goring
Did anyone deviate from norm — stopping, running, splitting wood without looking?

🎓 Key Principle:
Norm-deviation creates liability; mutual damage is netted, not stacked.

quiz.q: What does Rav add to the Mishnah's rule of the standing beam-carrier?
o: It only applies if he is a Levi
It applies only when he מְמַלָּא כָּל רְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים — fills the whole road
It applies only at night
It only applies to fragile vessels
quiz.e: Rav qualifies that the rule is for someone whose beam blocks the entire public way — and once he stops, נַעֲשֶׂה כְקֶרֶן זָוִית.

quiz.q: Why is Yose HaBavli also called Yose Katontah?
o: He was physically small
He was the youngest of his teachers
שֶׁהָיָה קְטָן חֲסִידִים — he was the last of the pious
He learned from a small school
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi explains the nickname: he was the smallest/last of the חסידים.

quiz.q: When is a runner in the public domain exempt according to Yose HaBavli?
o: Always
When he is running on his own property
עֶרֶב שַׁבָּת בֵּין הַשְּׁמָשׁוֹת — Friday twilight, when running is normal
Only if he warned passersby
quiz.e: Running is liable because שִׁינָּה הַמִּנְהָג; but at Friday twilight, hurrying is itself the customary norm.

quiz.q: How do R' Yose ben Chanina and R' Chiyya reconcile on the wood-splitter case?
o: They actually disagree fundamentally
לֹא פְלִיגֵי — R' Yose: when he saw the worker; R' Chiyya: when he didn't see
R' Yose was speaking of fire damage
R' Chiyya speaks only about animals
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi harmonizes them by case-splitting: visibility makes the difference between liability and exemption.

quiz.q: What is R' Yehudah's critique of R' Meir's reading of the verse?
o: R' Meir misread the Hebrew vowels
קִיַימְתָּה וּמָכְרוּ... וְלֹא קִיַימְתָּה וְגַם אֶת הַמֵּת יֶחֱצוּן — R' Meir upholds half the verse but ignores 'and they shall split the dead one too'
The verse doesn't apply to oxen
R' Meir's case never happens in practice
quiz.e: R' Yehudah insists every phrase of the pasuk must do halakhic work — each loses half the damage.
